metadata.note: Ideal nonrelativistic n=2 Stark model; parity and delta-m filters separated; real-hydrogen and line-intensity caveats explicit.

skin_warnings: B14: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. In ideal nonrelativistic hydrogen, four n-equals-two states share one energy. A weak static electric field mixes two of them and produces three first-order energy values.

props.command: How can four hydrogen states become three first-order energies?
props.runningText: diagonalizing the perturbation inside a degenerate subspace…
output: One independent s–p matrix element mixes two states.
Two other states remain unshifted to first order.

beats.narration_text: The new mixtures are lopsided along the field axis. One has an electric dipole aligned one way, the other the opposite way, so their energies shift linearly with opposite signs.
Which matrix elements survive, and why does one independent coupling control the first-order splitting?

Ignoring fine structure and the Lamb shift, the Coulomb Hamiltonian gives four degenerate states: two s, two p zero, and two p with magnetic quantum numbers plus and minus one. Real hydrogen lifts some of this degeneracy even before the field.
For a field along z, the perturbation is e script-E z. First-order degenerate perturbation theory means diagonalizing its four-by-four matrix inside this n-equals-two subspace.

Parity eliminates same-parity entries because z is odd. Azimuthal symmetry also enforces delta m equals zero, eliminating the s to p plus-or-minus-one couplings. Two conjugate matrix entries remain, but they are one independent real integral.

Diagonalizing the surviving two-by-two block gives equal superpositions of two s and two p zero. Their shifts are plus and minus three a-nought e script-E. The m equals plus-or-minus-one pair remains unshifted at first order.

The mixtures have opposite expectation values of z, so they carry opposite permanent dipole moments within this degenerate model. Energy minus dipole dot field then explains the linear, opposite shifts.

A nondegenerate state such as ideal hydrogen’s one-s ground state has no first-order permanent dipole by parity, so its leading Stark shift is quadratic. For n equals two, fine structure and the Lamb shift set a crossover: sufficiently weak fields require the more complete spectrum.

At ten to the fifth volts per centimeter, three a-nought e script-E is about sixteen millielectron-volts. That is the ideal first-order scale, before asking whether neglected splittings and experimental conditions matter.

Three first-order energy values do not automatically mean three observed lines with a two-to-one intensity. Line strengths depend on the initial and final states, dipole selection rules, populations, polarization, and viewing geometry.
Your turn. If the field is cut in half, what happens to the plus-or-minus first-order Stark shift in this linear regime?
It is cut in half. The sign still distinguishes the two lopsided mixtures, while the m equals plus-or-minus-one pair remains unshifted to first order.
Four States, Three First-Order Energies. Liam, in for Bear.
